article.title: OpenAI, AMD, Broadcom, Intel, Microsoft and Nvidia Publish MRC, an Ethernet-Based Networking Protocol Built for 100,000-GPU AI Clusters
article.summary: Multipath Reliable Connection extends RDMA over Ethernet with multipath transfers and microsecond failure recovery, and has been contributed to the Open Compute Project.
article.body_markdown:
## Overview

OpenAI, AMD, Broadcom, Intel, Microsoft and Nvidia have jointly published Multipath Reliable Connection (MRC), a new networking protocol designed to keep training jobs running across the kind of GPU clusters now being built for frontier AI models. The specification was contributed to the Open Compute Project, according to [Network World](https://www.networkworld.com/article/4168926/openai-led-consortium-seeks-to-address-ai-processing-bottlenecks.html) and [The Decoder](https://the-decoder.com/openai-built-a-networking-protocol-with-amd-broadcom-intel-microsoft-and-nvidia-to-fix-ai-supercomputer-bottlenecks/), and is accompanied by a research paper with contributions from all five partner organisations alongside OpenAI.

The protocol distributes a single data transfer across hundreds of network paths simultaneously and reroutes around failures on a microsecond timescale, where conventional Ethernet fabrics can take seconds to tens of seconds to stabilise after a link or device failure, as reported by [The Decoder](https://the-decoder.com/openai-built-a-networking-protocol-with-amd-broadcom-intel-microsoft-and-nvidia-to-fix-ai-supercomputer-bottlenecks/).

## What MRC Does

MRC is built into emerging 800Gb/s network interfaces and is designed for training models on more than 100,000 GPUs, according to [Network World](https://www.networkworld.com/article/4168926/openai-led-consortium-seeks-to-address-ai-processing-bottlenecks.html). The same source notes that the protocol can connect more than 100,000 GPUs using only two tiers of Ethernet switches, versus the three to four tiers required for standard 800Gb/s networks. Reducing the number of switch tiers cuts power, component count and cost, [The Decoder](https://the-decoder.com/openai-built-a-networking-protocol-with-amd-broadcom-intel-microsoft-and-nvidia-to-fix-ai-supercomputer-bottlenecks/) reports.

Technically, MRC extends the existing RDMA over Converged Ethernet stack rather than replacing it. Analyst Zeus Kerravala, writing for [SiliconANGLE](https://siliconangle.com/2026/05/06/nvidias-mrc-just-ethernet-isnt-enough-gigascale-ai/), describes MRC as "an RDMA transport that lets a single connection fan out across multiple network paths, using multipath awareness, congestion hints and fast retransmission." The same article reports that MRC builds on RoCEv2 as defined by the InfiniBand Trade Association, and extends it with multipath, host-governed routing and multi-plane integration.

Four capabilities are listed in the SiliconANGLE write-up: multipath load balancing across network paths, congestion-aware routing using real-time fabric signals, fast and precise retransmission for lost data, and microsecond failure bypass and rerouting in hardware. Network World notes that the protocol gives operators visibility and control over traffic paths through a single dashboard.

## Where It Is Already Running

MRC is not a forward-looking specification. It is already deployed inside several of the largest AI training environments operating today.

According to [The Decoder](https://the-decoder.com/openai-built-a-networking-protocol-with-amd-broadcom-intel-microsoft-and-nvidia-to-fix-ai-supercomputer-bottlenecks/), MRC operates on OpenAI's largest Nvidia GB200 supercomputers, including the Oracle Cloud Infrastructure site in Abilene, Texas, and Microsoft's Fairwater supercomputers. [Network World](https://www.networkworld.com/article/4168926/openai-led-consortium-seeks-to-address-ai-processing-bottlenecks.html) names the Abilene facility as part of Project Stargate.

[SiliconANGLE](https://siliconangle.com/2026/05/06/nvidias-mrc-just-ethernet-isnt-enough-gigascale-ai/) reports that OpenAI has deployed MRC on Spectrum-X to train "frontier large language models powering ChatGPT and Codex," and that Microsoft is deploying it in some of its largest AI factories built on GB200 systems. Network World separately notes that MRC incorporates Nvidia's Spectrum-X Ethernet and uses a multi-plane network design.

One operational data point from [The Decoder](https://the-decoder.com/openai-built-a-networking-protocol-with-amd-broadcom-intel-microsoft-and-nvidia-to-fix-ai-supercomputer-bottlenecks/) illustrates what microsecond rerouting buys in practice: during a recent frontier model training run, OpenAI rebooted four tier-1 switches without requiring coordination with training teams, something The Decoder describes as previously impossible without MRC.

## Why It Matters

As model training shifts toward clusters of more than 100,000 GPUs that can be fully connected with only two tiers of Ethernet switches, fabric instability becomes a more expensive problem to ignore. Routing the specification through the Open Compute Project rather than releasing it as a single-vendor protocol mirrors the pattern of recent multi-vendor industry collaborations on AI hardware standards. Whether infrastructure providers outside the original consortium adopt MRC will be the next signal of how far that consensus extends.

## What We Don't Know

None of the four reports surveyed disclose pricing, licensing terms beyond the OCP contribution, throughput benchmarks against the InfiniBand baselines that MRC is competing with, or a public timeline for when non-consortium vendors will ship MRC-capable interfaces. The protocol's research paper, published alongside the specification through the Open Compute Project, is referenced but its full benchmark methodology was not summarised in the press coverage examined for this report.
